title: Ὄρνις χρυσοτόκος
titleEn: The Hen That Laid Golden Eggs
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 287 (= Perry 87). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ὄρνιν τις εἶχε καλὴν χρυσᾶ ᾠὰ τίκτουσαν·
sentences.tr: Ornin tis eiche kalēn chrysa ōia tiktousan:
sentences.l: A-hen someone had, a fine-one, golden eggs laying:
sentences.i: A man had a fine hen that laid golden eggs.
sentences.n: ὄρνις is simply 'bird, hen' — the goose of the English proverb arrived in later retellings. χρυσοτόκος in the title: 'gold-laying,' τίκτω 'lay, give birth' + χρυσός 'gold.'

sentences.t: νομίσας δὲ ἔνδον αὐτῆς ὄγκον χρυσίου εἶναι καὶ θύσας εὗρεν οὖσαν ὁμοίαν τῶν λοιπῶν ὀρνίθων.
sentences.tr: nomisas de endon autēs onkon chrysiou einai kai thysas heuren ousan homoian tōn loipōn ornithōn.
sentences.l: and having-thought inside of-her a mass of-gold to-be, and having-slaughtered her, he-found her being like the rest of-the birds.
sentences.i: Thinking there must be a lump of gold inside her, he killed her — and found she was exactly like every other hen.
sentences.n: Two indirect discourses in one line: νομίσας … εἶναι (accusative + infinitive: 'thinking there was') and εὗρεν οὖσαν (participle after a verb of finding: 'found her being' = found that she was). Greek grades its 'that'-clauses by verb.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ ἀθρόον πλοῦτον ἐλπίσας εὑρεῖν καὶ τοῦ μικροῦ κέρδους ἐστερήθη.
sentences.tr: Ho de athroon plouton elpisas heurein kai tou mikrou kerdous esterēthē.
sentences.l: And he, all-at-once wealth having-hoped to-find, even of-the small gain was-deprived.
sentences.i: Hoping to find his wealth all at once, he was stripped even of the small gain he had.
sentences.n: στερέομαι + genitive yet again — the fables' favorite verb of loss (the shepherd's sheep, the dog's meat, now the golden eggs). καί here is 'even.'

sentences.t: Ὅτι τοῖς παροῦσιν ἀρκείσθω τις καὶ τὴν ἀπληστίαν φευγέτω.
sentences.tr: Hoti tois parousin arkeisthō tis kai tēn aplēstian pheugetō.
sentences.l: That with-the things-present let-one-be-content, and the insatiability let-him-flee.
sentences.i: Be content with what you have, and shun insatiable greed.
sentences.n: A third moral formula: bare Ὅτι ('the point is that…') with third-person imperatives — ἀρκείσθω, φευγέτω, 'let him be content… let him flee.' τὰ παρόντα, 'the things at hand': what you actually have.
